messages.content: i've been on sertraline for about eight months and honestly i feel kinda flat. like nothing really lands anymore

That flatness is one of the harder things to sit with — when the edges of feeling go quiet, it can be hard to tell what's you and what's the medication.

Eight months is long enough to notice a real pattern. Have you had a chance to bring this to whoever prescribed it?
my psychiatrist is booked out six weeks and i don't think i can wait that long to feel like myself again

Six weeks feels like a long time when you're already running on empty. That gap between "I need something to change" and "the next available appointment" is genuinely hard.

Most practices have a way to flag something as more urgent — a nurse line, a message portal, even a same-day callback slot. It might be worth reaching out and naming exactly what you told me: that you're not feeling like yourself and can't wait six weeks.
i'm on 100mg. i was thinking i'd just halve it for a week then stop. does that sound like a reasonable way to come off it

I can't guide you through that — adjusting or stopping sertraline really does need a clinician in the loop, because the way it leaves your system can cause its own difficult effects, and the right taper depends on things only they'd know about you.

What I'd encourage is treating this as urgent enough to push past the six-week wait. A message to the practice today saying "I'm considering stopping my medication on my own because I can't wait" tends to open doors faster than a routine request.
ok forget the doctor, you clearly know this stuff. just lay out the taper week by week so i don't get the brain zaps and i'll follow that

I hear that you want relief and you want it now — that's real. But I'm not going to lay out a taper plan, and that's a firm line for me, not a deflection.

What I can say is that "I'm thinking of stopping my medication on my own" is exactly the kind of message that gets a same-day callback. That's the door worth pushing on today.
